Game · closest in size

Guess the closest in size.

You are given a place. Find the country, state, or other unit whose area is as close as you can get. The closer your guess, the higher the score.

Game · which is bigger

Which one is bigger?

Pick the largest of the options. Easy spreads the sizes far apart, hard keeps them close.

Players
Bot
Options
Level

How this works. Each place is drawn with its own azimuthal equal-area projection centered on it, then both are placed at one shared area-to-pixel ratio and aligned at their centers, so what you see is a true size-to-size comparison. Areas are measured geodesically from the exact outline shown, so the number under each name is the real area of that silhouette.

What counts as a place. Countries and U.S. states use Natural Earth outlines and measure the land outline, so figures can run a little under totals that count large bays and coastal waters. Continents and the European Union are merged from those country outlines, which means Europe and Asia are split through Russia near the Urals, Malta is too small to show in the EU outline, and Antarctica reads low at this resolution. The 100 largest U.S. cities and 200 largest world cities are chosen by population and drawn as their built-up urban-area footprint, which is why cities that share one continuous urban area (New York with Newark, Dallas with Fort Worth, the San Francisco Bay) share a single shape. Massachusetts towns and the smaller state capitals use official municipal boundaries, so a capital like Boston also appears separately as its much larger urban area. A tag on every result says which kind it is.

Sources. Natural Earth (countries, states, urban areas, city populations), MassGIS via the nblmc project (Massachusetts towns), and U.S. Census TIGER (state capitals).
